title: Malignant Salivary Gland Tumors -- Medical Oncology
content: Targeted therapies against C-Kit (Imatinib), HER2 (Trastuzumab), EGFR (Cetuximab), and mTOR (Temsirolimus) have gained popularity due to the expression of biological receptors in several malignant salivary tumors. However, response rates have not been superior. This is likely due to a discrepancy between protein overexpression and gene mutation and loss of tumor suppressor PTEN that interferes with target therapy responsiveness. title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
content: Phosphatase and Tensin Homolog ( PTEN ) Gene: PTEN gene encodes a lipid phosphatase that negatively regulates the phosphoinositide-3-kinase (PI3K)-AKT and the target of mTOR signaling pathways. These pathways are vital for cell proliferation, cell cycle progression, and apoptosis. [24] The PTEN protein also functions to keep migration, adhesion, and angiogenesis in check. It also plays a role in the overall stabilization of the genome. A biallelic loss-of-function is common in diverse cancers. Cowden syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder resulting from germline loss-of-function mutations of this gene and correlates with a higher risk of breast and endometrial cancer

title: Tubular Adenoma -- Differential Diagnosis
content: Cowden syndrome and PTEN hamartoma: PTEN acts as a tumor suppressor gene. Cowden syndrome, as an autosomal dominant syndrome and related to PTEN hamartoma tumor syndrome (PHTS), has a broad range of clinical manifestations, including trichilemmomas in the face and malignant pathologies in the breast, thyroid, and gastrointestinal polyps. Obtaining timely screening schedules to exclude malignancies in these patients is crucial. Traditionally, there was a remarkable 80% germline PTEN mutation in patients affected by Cowden syndrome; however, recently, the specificity of these criteria has been questioned. [23]

title: Dermatofibrosarcoma Protuberans -- Treatment / Management -- Molecularly Targeted Therapy
content: Imatinib inhibits the PDGF receptor and other receptor tyrosine kinases such as c-KIT. Imatinib has the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval for unresectable, recurrent, or metastatic DFSP. [27] Imatinib competitively inhibits adenosine triphosphate (ATP) binding to the PDGFB receptor, slowing down kinase activity, limiting tumor growth, and promoting apoptosis. Patients with the t(17;22)(q22;q13) translocation show a more significant response to imatinib, and thus, screening for this translocation should be performed before initiating therapy. Further studies are necessary to see if patients without the translocation will benefit from imatinib.

title: Renal Clear Cell Cancer -- Etiology
content: While most of the cases of clear cell carcinoma are sporadic, 2 to 3% of the cases are related to genetic alterations in the von Hippel landau gene, chromatin remodeling genes/epigenetic regulators (Polybromo 1, BRCA protein 1, and SET D1), and genetic alterations in pathways that disrupt signal transduction involving the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway. [16] [17] PTEN deficiency is usually associated with a more aggressive phenotype. [18] While angiomyolipoma (AML) is the most common renal neoplasm seen in patients with tuberous sclerosis, clear cell tumors may be seen rarely. [19] Clear cell tumors can also be seen in a small minority of patients with Birt Hogg Dube syndrome, who have been otherwise shown to develop hybrid-chromophobe tumors and oncocytomas. [20] [21]

title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Points to Remember
content: Tumor suppressor genes function to either repress or inhibit the cell cycle or promote apoptosis. The more specific functions of tumor suppressor proteins fall into several categories, including [1] [21] : Inhibition of mitogenic signaling pathways Inhibition of cell cycle progression Inhibition of “pro-growth” programs of metabolism and angiogenesis Inhibition of invasion and metastasis Stabilization of the genome DNA repair factors Induction of apoptosis

title: Vemurafenib -- Indications
content: The kinase protein family, including MAP, RAS, RAF, MEK, and ERK, has a key role in regulating intracellular and extracellular signaling pathways that control cellular growth, differentiation, transformation, and apoptosis (programmed cell death). In normal cells, these kinases operate in a controlled cascade of phosphorylation and dephosphorylation in response to growth factors, eventually leading to the regulation of cellular growth and proliferation in an organized manner. However, mutations in the genes encoding these kinases can disrupt this regulation, resulting in uncontrolled cell growth, which can lead to the development of cancer.

title: Mohs Micrographic Surgery Risks: Carcinogenicity of Immunosuppressive Agents -- Issues of Concern -- Cyclosporine
content: Cyclosporine is an immunosuppressive calcineurin inhibitor that works by binding to cyclophilin, inhibiting calcineurin, and reducing T-lymphocyte activation to suppress the immune response. In addition to impairing the immune system's ability to detect and fight cancer, cyclosporine also has carcinogenic properties through the suppression of PTEN, which leads to subsequent activation of the AKT pathway. [21] Studies have demonstrated a significant increase in squamous cell carcinoma incidence during cyclosporine treatment. In a randomized trial involving 231 posttransplant patients, a comparison of low- and high-dose cyclosporine revealed a dose-dependent increase in the development of squamous cell carcinomas. [22]

title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Pathophysiology
content: LGGs grow slowly and can be followed over the years without treatment unless they cause symptoms and grow. Multiple acquired genetic mutations are found in gliomas. Tumor suppressor protein 53 (p53), phosphatase and tensin homolog (PTEN), and epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) are involved in the pathogenesis of these tumors. p53 is the "guardian of the genome" and ensures the DNA is copied correctly; it destroys the cell if it is mutated. p53 usually mutates early in the disease, and other mutations can survive. EGFR normally stimulates cells to divide, but if it gets amplified, it stimulates cells to divide too much. Together these mutations cause uncontrollable cell division, a hallmark of cancer. [9]

title: Cowden Disease -- Pathophysiology
content: The protein encoded by the PTEN gene contributes to the control of apoptosis and the cell cycle. [12] Specifically, the phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PI3K)/AKT/mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway is down-regulated by the PTEN gene product, resulting in decreased cellular proliferation and survival. [1] When heterozygosity for the PTEN gene is lost by a "second hit" mutation, the resulting phenotype of multiple hamartomas and neoplasms is produced. [13] Most commonly, patients experience macrocephaly along with mucocutaneous lesions such as oral papillomas, tricholemmomas, and acral keratoses.

title: Adrenocortical Cancer -- Prognosis
content: Recently, transcriptome analysis revealed unraveled two distinct groups of adrenocortical carcinoma, reflecting tumor proliferation (measured by mitotic counts and cell cycle genes): one group of aggressive proliferating tumors (C1A), associated with a poor prognosis and one group of less aggressive tumors (C1B), associated with a better outcome. Multivariate analysis, including tumor stage and mitotic rate as variables, showed that transcriptome data contained independent prognosis information. One of these studies also used the transcriptome data to define a genes predictor of malignancy and identified three different subgroups in the group of ACC of poor prognosis: one was enriched in TP53 mutations (C1A/p53), the second in beta-catenin cytoplasmic and nuclear accumulation (C1A/beta-catenin) and/or mutations and the third presented neither of these two alterations (C1A/x). [36] The delineation of these three subgroups relied on different tumorigenesis processes, but they were equally associated with a poor prognosis. More recently, another study reported a global hypomethylation pattern in ACC associated with hypermethylation of specific promoter regions responsible for the downregulation of the expression of a certain number of genes. [18] A third study reported a negative correlation between methylation and expression for a total of 1741 genes of 12250 studied, among the top genes were found H19 and other tumor suppressor genes. [16] This last study further used unsupervised clustering of DNA methylation profiles, unraveling two groups of carcinomas, one with an elevated methylation level, in favor of a CpG island methylator phenotype (CIMP) and the other with a methylation level only slightly increased in comparison to adenomas (non-CIMP). CIMP group could further subdivide into two subgroups with two different methylation levels (CIMP high and CIMP low). Hypermethylation was associated with poor survival; this received further confirmation in a recent study, which showed that tumor methylation status was a significant prognostic factor of disease-free survival (DFS) and overall survival (OS). [19] They identified a strong methylation marker, taking into account the mean methylation of 4 genes (PAX5, GSTP1, PYCARD, and PAX6). More interestingly, they showed that this biomarker remained a significant prognostic factor for DFS and OS in multivariate analysis, including ENSAT stage and Ki67. This biomarker seems particularly attractive; most of the previous reported molecular markers only had investigation using univariate analysis of survival. [19] Integrative approaches confronting transcriptome and methylome data found that CIMP-high phenotype was only present in C1A/x and C1A/p53 tumors while non-CIMP phenotype appeared in C1A/beta-catenin and C1B tumors. [16] [1]

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
content: Dasatinib and dasatinib anhydrous are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a class of targeted therapies that block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase activity implicated in certain leukemias. By inhibiting this oncogenic kinase, both the standard and anhydrous formulations of dasatinib help to control malignant cell proliferation and are used in the management of chronic myeloid leukemia and Philadelphia chromosome‑positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Recognizing dasatinib and its anhydrous form as BCR‑ABL inhibitors underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in precision oncology.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
content: Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.
